stem: For \( y'' + ([[b]])y' + ([[c]])y = [[m]]x+[[n]] \), the method of undetermined coefficients uses the trial particular solution \( y_p = Ax+B \). Find the coefficient \( A \).
solution: Substituting \( y_p=Ax+B \) gives \( y_p''+([[b]])y_p'+([[c]])y_p = ([[b]])A+([[c]])(Ax+B) \). Matching the coefficient of \( x \) gives \( ([[c]])A=[[m]] \), so \( A = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This matches the constant term instead of the coefficient of \( x \).
This divides by the wrong coefficient \( b \) instead of \( c \).
This has the wrong sign.
This multiplies instead of dividing by \( c \).
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: The forcing rate does NOT match a characteristic root, so for \( y'' + ([[b]])y' + ([[c]])y = [[d]]e^{[[k]]x} \), the trial particular solution is \( y_p = Ae^{[[k]]x} \). Find the coefficient \( A \).
distractor_rationales: This has the wrong sign on the middle term of the denominator.
This drops the \( k \)-dependent part of the denominator entirely.
This has the wrong overall sign.
This multiplies by the denominator instead of dividing.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: For \( y'' + ([[b]])y' + ([[c]])y = [[d]]\cos([[k]]x) \), the trial particular solution is \( y_p = A\cos([[k]]x)+B\sin([[k]]x) \). Matching coefficients of \( \cos([[k]]x) \) and \( \sin([[k]]x) \) gives a linear system for \( A \) and \( B \). Find \( A \).
solution: Matching \( \cos([[k]]x) \) and \( \sin([[k]]x) \) coefficients gives \( ([[c]]-[[k]]^{2})A+([[b]])[[k]]B=[[d]] \) and \( -([[b]])[[k]]A+([[c]]-[[k]]^{2})B=0 \). Solving this system, \( A = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores the coupling from the \( \sin \) equation, using only the naive single-equation quotient.
This reports the value of \( B \), not \( A \).
This has the wrong overall sign for \( A \).
This ignores the \( k^2 \) shift in the denominator entirely.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: The forcing rate does NOT match a characteristic root, so for \( y'' + ([[b]])y' + ([[c]])y = [[d]]xe^{[[k]]x} \), the trial particular solution is \( y_p = (Ax+B)e^{[[k]]x} \). Matching the coefficients of \( xe^{[[k]]x} \) and \( e^{[[k]]x} \) gives a linear system for \( A \) and \( B \). Find \( A \).
solution: Matching the coefficient of \( xe^{[[k]]x} \) gives \( ([[k]]^{2}+([[b]])[[k]]+([[c]]))A = [[d]] \), so \( A = [[answer]] \) (the second equation only determines \( B \)).
distractor_rationales: This has the wrong overall sign.
This uses the coefficient equation meant for \( B \) instead of \( A \).
This multiplies by the denominator instead of dividing.
This ignores the \( k \)-dependence of the denominator entirely.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: The forcing term of \( y'' + ([[b]])y' + ([[c]])y = [[d1]]e^{[[k1]]x} + [[d2]] \) is a sum of two pieces, so by superposition the particular solution is \( y_p = A_1 e^{[[k1]]x} + A_2 \), where each coefficient is found by matching ONLY its own forcing term. Find \( A_1 \), the coefficient of the exponential piece.
solution: By superposition, solve for each forcing term separately. For \( [[d1]]e^{[[k1]]x} \), try \( A_1 e^{[[k1]]x} \): substituting gives \( A_1 = \dfrac{[[d1]]}{[[k1]]^{2}+([[b]])[[k1]]+([[c]])} = [[answer]] \) (the constant forcing term [[d2]] only affects \( A_2 \), found separately).
distractor_rationales: This solves for the coefficient of the CONSTANT forcing piece instead of the exponential piece.
This mixes the two forcing terms together instead of treating them separately by superposition.
This has the wrong overall sign.
This uses the denominator for the constant-forcing piece instead of the exponential one.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.
